structure.title: INTRODUCTION
structure.text:
INTRODUCTION   
Convolutional neural networks are widely used image for 
classification and object recogni tion tasks. Convolution layer is 
responsible for extracting important features with the help of 
filters or feature extractor. A deep neural network has been 
implemented to perform image classification on CIFAR-10 
dataset. Various hyperparameters need to be tuned to extract 
best performance from the network. To avoid overfitting, we have tried to reduce the model complexity by using dropout 
and tried to increase data size by using data augmentation. The 
choice of function optimizer is very important since it directly 
affects convergence speed. Adam optimizer has been used in 
the network. ReLU activation function is used throughout the 
network except at the output layer where Softmax activation 
function is used. The output labels are in the form of integers 
and need to be changed to categorical using one hot encoding. 
During training each forward pass results in an output value which is compared with the actual output and loss function is 
computed. Cross-entropy loss function is used along with 
Softmax activation at the output and the gradient of the loss 
function are propagated back into the network to optimize the 
weights. This process is repeated for fixed number of epochs to 
get best possible accuracy. 
 
 
II. T HE DATASET   
CIFAR-10 dataset is used for training the network.
structure.summary: Convolutional neural networks are used for image classification and object recognition tasks, with convolution layers extracting features using filters. A deep neural network was implemented for image classification on the CIFAR-10 dataset, utilizing techniques such as dropout, data augmentation, and the Adam optimizer to prevent overfitting and improve performance. The network uses ReLU and Softmax activation functions, cross-entropy loss function, and one hot encoding to achieve optimal accuracy.

structure.title: THE DATASET
structure.text:
The dataset 
consists of 60000, 32x32 color images divided into 10 classes. 
Each class has 6000 images. The images are further divided into training and test set with each set having 50000 and 10000 
images respectively. The CIFAR-10 classes are: Airplane, 
Automobile, Bird, Cat, Deer, Dog, Frog, Horse, Ship, Truck.  
Leaky ReLu [8][9] is a nonlinear Activation function. It is an 
important part of our neural network architecture since it 
introduces non-linearity to the ne twork. In the absence of 
nonlinear activation function deep neural net would behave like a single layered network since summing these layers would just 
give us another linear function. Neural networks are considered 
universal function approximators. The use of nonlinear 
activation function and hidden layers gives it an ability to 
approximate complex functions. There is a choice of using 
various activation functions like, Sigmoid, Tanh, ReLu, Leaky 
ReLu etc. Sigmoid function has non-zero mean and during 
backpropagation through the netw ork, gradients in neurons 
whose output is near 0 or 1 are nearly 0. 
 
 
Hence these neurons are called saturated neurons. The weights 
of these neurons are not updated, and weights of the neurons connected to these are updated slowly. This is known as 
vanishing gradient. Moreover, these activation functions are computationally expensive. Hyperbolic tangent activation function also suffers from vanishing gradient but is slightly 
better than sigmoid activation function since it is zero centered 
and has 0 mean. These saturating  nonlinear functions lead to 
slower training with gradient  descent as compared to non-
saturating nonlinear functions like ReLu and leaky ReLu. 
 
ReLu activation function makes the network converge much 
faster as it does not saturate (when x > 0) and is computationally efficient. It is defined as, f(x) = max (0, x) but it suffers from a drawback, when x < 0, during forward pass neurons remain inactive and weights are not updated during backpropagation. As a result, the network does not learn. Hence, we use leaky ReLu [8] which is defined as 
 
f(x) = max ( αx, x) (1) 
Here, since for x<0 the function has a small finite 
slope the derivation is no longer 0 for x<0 and weights are updated during backpropagation.

structure.summary: The dataset consists of 60,000 32x32 color images divided into 10 classes, with 50,000 images for training and 10,000 for testing. The neural network architecture utilizes a leaky ReLu activation function, which introduces non-linearity and allows the network to approximate complex functions. The use of leaky ReLu helps to avoid vanishing gradients and saturated neurons, enabling faster convergence and more efficient training.

structure.title: THE ARCHITECTURE

nodes.title: Leaky ReLu Activation Function
nodes.text:
Leaky ReLu Activation Function  
Keywords—Convolution, Max-pooling, CIFAR-10 
 
I.
nodes.summary: The Leaky ReLu activation function is utilized in conjunction with convolution and max-pooling techniques. The function is applied to the CIFAR-10 dataset, a standard benchmark for image classification tasks. The Leaky ReLu activation function allows a small fraction of the input to pass through, helping to avoid dying neurons during training.

nodes.title: Convolutional layers
nodes.text:
Convolutional layers  
The training data used for training the deep neural network  
[9] consists of images of objects from 10 different classes. 
Pixels in image data form patterns and rearranging the 
rows or columns of images would break this pattern. 
Convolutional neural networks only capture local spatial patterns in data. Convolutional nets are great at finding patterns and using them to classify images.  
Convolution layer consist of  input image and a stack of 
filters/features. A 2D convolution is performed between 
image array and filter resulting in a stack of filtered image arrays at the output. The filtered images consist of 
numbers representing how well the filter matched that part 
of the image. This becomes the map of where the feature occurs. This act of convolving an image with a bunch of filters and creating a stack of filtered images is called 
convolution layer. The input to first convolution layer in our 
network is a single image and output is a stack of 32 filtered images. The network has 6 convolution layers with 
pooling layers stacked in between. 
 
C.
nodes.summary: Convolutional neural networks capture local spatial patterns in image data by performing 2D convolutions between the image array and a stack of filters. The output of a convolution layer is a stack of filtered image arrays, where each filtered image represents how well a filter matched a part of the original image. The network consists of 6 convolution layers with pooling layers in between, taking a single image as input and producing a stack of 32 filtered images at the first convolution layer.

nodes.title: Batch Normalization
nodes.text:
Batch Normalization  
First layer of the deep network has training data as input which 
does not change during training, but hidden layers take the output of previous layers as input, which changes as the 
previous layers weights are optimized during backpropagation. 
Thus, we can view the input to ea ch layer as a shifting dataset 
of hidden activations during training [9]. Covariate shift in the input data results in retraining of 
network. A shifting input causes convergence of weights during training to become slower. Batch normalization 
reduces the amount by which the distribution of these 
hidden units shifts around. The mean and variance of the data input to hidden layers does not change much after 
applying batch normalization layer. 
 
 
 
∑ Փ 
 
Add Batch-Norm 
 
 
∑ Փ   
    B  
 
 
Fig 2: Batch normalization layer  
We add an additional layer that normalizes in 
batch wise fashion. Batch-norm parameters become optimized during training. Batch-norm has the effect of increasing the training speed. 
 
The ith unit contains in Batch-norm layer contains 
two parameters βi and γi that need to be learned.  
Steps in batch normalization [9]:  
1. Normalize over batch of m instances for ith unit.

Most activations will be near 0 (near linear region), 
γ and β provide the ability to each layer to decide 
how much non-linearity they want. 
 
D.
nodes.summary: Batch normalization reduces the shift in the distribution of hidden units during training by normalizing the input to each layer in a batch-wise fashion. The process involves calculating the batch mean and variance, normalizing the batch instances, and scaling with learnable parameters β and γ. This results in increased training speed and allows each layer to decide its desired level of non-linearity.

nodes.title: Max Pooling layer
nodes.text:
Max Pooling layer  
Max pooling layers [1] are applied in the network 
architecture after every two convolution layers. There 
are 3 pooling layers in total with size of 2x2. These 
pooling layers shrink the image stack by retaining the 
maximum value. Max Pooling is applied on entire stack of filtered images to get smaller stack of filtered images. 
 
E.
nodes.summary: Max pooling layers are applied after every two convolution layers, with three pooling layers in total, each with a size of 2x2. These layers reduce the image stack size by retaining the maximum value from the entire stack of filtered images. The max pooling process results in a smaller stack of filtered images.

nodes.title: Loss Function
nodes.text:
Loss Function  
The activation function used in the convolution layers and 
hidden layers is ReLU which works well for those layers. The output layers need to classify images into 10 different 
classes and uses Softmax activation function. Softmax 
function forces output of the neural network to sum to 1 so that they can represent a probability distribution across a 
bunch of discrete mutually exclusive alternatives.

depend on z i but also on z j, accumulated by other nodes.  
Here, y i represents the probability distribution over mutually 
exclusive alternatives just by using Softmax equation. The right cost function to use with Softmax activation is Cross- 
entropy [9].

nodes.summary: The activation functions used in the neural network are ReLU for convolution and hidden layers, and Softmax for output layers to classify images into 10 classes. The Softmax function ensures the output represents a probability distribution, and the Cross-entropy cost function is used with Softmax activation. The Cross-entropy cost function has a large gradient when the target value is 1 and the output is near 0, and its slope decreases as the difference between actual and target values decreases.

structure.text:
The architecture of the convolutional neural network 
is summarized in figure 1. The network has 6 convolution layers, 3 pooling layers and 3 fully 
connected layers. Below is a discussion of some of 
the important features of the network architecture 
 
A.
structure.summary: The convolutional neural network architecture consists of 6 convolution layers, 3 pooling layers, and 3 fully connected layers. The architecture is summarized in figure 1, which highlights key features of the network. The network's design includes multiple layers for convolution, pooling, and fully connected operations.

structure.title: REDUCING OVERFITTING

nodes.title: Regularization
nodes.text:
regularization or increase 
dataset by methods su ch as data augmentation. One way to 
implement regularization is to add a term to our cost function that penalizes overly complex models. Overfitted neural 
network is unable to generalize well. It learns the features of 
training data well but when we input test data which deviates from training data, the system doesn’t give good results. A large dataset gives more diversity to our model. When the 
performance on unseen data is  not as good as performance on 
unseen data, there is a generalization gap between performance to train and test data. 
 
Table 1: Classification of loss curves [9] 
Training loss Validation loss  
High High Underfitting
Low High Overfitting
Low Low Good fit
High low Unlikely 
 
An underfitted data has low variance but high bias, 
whereas overfitted model may have low bias but high variance. Our model initially had a low training loss but high validation loss indicating Overfitting. The solution 
to overfitting is to reduce capacity by regularization and
nodes.summary: Regularization methods, such as data augmentation and adding a penalty term to the cost function, can prevent overfitting in neural networks. Overfitting occurs when a model learns the training data too well and fails to generalize to new data, resulting in a generalization gap between training and test performance. A model is considered well-fit when it has low training and validation loss, whereas underfitting and overfitting are characterized by high training and validation loss, and low training but high validation loss, respectively.

nodes.title: Dropout
nodes.text:
Dropout and increase data by data augmentation. A. Regularization  
Encourages models to have a preference towards simpler 
models without making a part of the hypothesis space completely unavailable. These techniques intend to reduce the risk of overfitting without increasing bias significantly. 
 
In regularization, 
Loss 
  Loss + (Weight penalty) 
 
Weight decay term puts a constraint on the model 
by penalizing large weights.  
∑ f (y i – y i’) + α∑ (5) 
α represents regularization strength and is a 
hyperparameter used to tune our model [9]. 
 
B. Dropout  
Dropout [7] randomly drops out neurons with some probability 
during training. The training al gorithm uses random subset of 
the network at every iteration. This approach encourages 
neurons to learn useful features on their own without relying on 
other neurons. Once trained the entire network is used for inference. To prevent overfitting we have two options, we can 
either reduce the effective capacity of the network by using 
methods such as dropout or we  can increase the amount of 
training data by generating modified versions of the samples 
we already have using data augmentation. 
 
 
V. F UNCTION  OPTIMIZATION   
In
nodes.summary: Dropout and regularization techniques are used to reduce the risk of overfitting in models. Regularization methods, such as weight decay, add a penalty term to the loss function to constrain the model and prevent large weights, with the regularization strength controlled by a hyperparameter. Dropout randomly drops out neurons during training to encourage individual neurons to learn useful features without relying on others, and can be used in conjunction with data augmentation to prevent overfitting.

structure.text:
R EDUCING OVERFITTING  
To check overfitting, the data is splitted into two portions, 
validation data and training data. The error is plotted on the 
training and testing stats as the model is trained. A large difference in the error graph on the training and validation dataset indicated overfitting. To reduce overfitting, either 
reduce model complexity using
structure.summary: Data is split into training and validation portions to check for overfitting. The error is plotted on both datasets during model training, with a large difference indicating overfitting. To reduce overfitting, model complexity is reduced.

structure.title: FUNCTION OPTIMIZATION

nodes.title: Stochastic gradient descent
nodes.text:
Stochastic gradient descent  
This is like gradient descent except the wei ghts are updated 
only on one example instead of the entire set. But this method 
leads to noisy updates and convergence is not smooth. A better alternative to SGD method is mini-batch gradient method where 
weights are updated on a mini-batch which is a subset of the 
entire set. Here we have m points to estimate gradient instead of 1 point in SGD. Stochastic and mini-batch descents use 
gradient approximations to speedup computations, but suitable 
learning rate is hard to select. For difficult functions gradient descent oscillates and it takes many iterations for it to converge 
to optimum weights [9].

taken in the direction determined not only by the current gradient but all the previous gradients as well. This 
prevents fluctuations and oscillations as seen in SGD.

removing overfitting  VII. C ONCLUSION  
In this paper we have implemented a Convolutional neural 
network to classify images into 10 categories. CIFAR-10 dataset has been used as a benchmark to train the network. The Adam optimization technique to update weights gives us the best accuracy in terms of image classification. The model overfitting is reduced by using regularization and dropout methods. Using deep network architecture, we were able to achieve 90% accuracy on the training data and 87.57 % accuracy on the test set.
nodes.summary: Stochastic gradient descent updates weights on one example instead of the entire set, but leads to noisy updates, and a better alternative is the mini-batch gradient method. The Adam optimization technique is used to update weights, giving the best accuracy in terms of image classification, with 90% accuracy on the training data and 87.57% accuracy on the test set. The model's overfitting is reduced by using regularization, dropout methods, and data augmentation.

structure.text:
Function optimization we try to minimize the loss 
function by finding the optimum parameters of the network. Most common method to find the minimum is gradient 
descent. This is a first order method which finds the slope 
of the tangent of the graph of the loss function w.r.t weights. Gradient descent optimization problem [9]: 
 
L(w) = ∑     (  ;  , ) min w 
W0 = initialization   
Weights update equation for gradient descent is:
Wt = wt-1 - α    (   ) (6)  
In this method l gradients are computed on each step and 
hence this method is a bit slower since l can be very large. 
Gradient descent is infeasible for large training sets. 
 
A.
structure.summary: Function optimization involves minimizing the loss function by finding the optimum parameters of the network, typically using the gradient descent method. Gradient descent is a first-order method that finds the slope of the tangent of the loss function with respect to weights, with the weights update equation given by Wt = wt-1 - α. This method can be slow and infeasible for large training sets due to the computation of gradients at each step.

structure.summary: The references cited include research on deep learning, stochastic optimization, and adaptive learning rate methods by authors such as Goodfellow, Kingma, and Zeiler. These references cover various topics including improving neural networks, gradient-based learning, and convolutional networks, and were published in outlets like arXiv, IEEE, and ICML. The cited works also include online resources like deeplearning.ai and research on techniques like dropout training and rectified linear units.
